macro_segments.title: Rabbi Eliezer's Position on Conditional Divorce and Rabbinic Responses

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Rabbi Eliezer's Strange Position: Divorce Excluding One Person
Rabbi Shimon ben Elazar's Response: The Woman Marries Someone Else
The Problem of Undoing Previous Restrictions: Yevamah, Vows, and Husband's Power to Void Vows
Rabbi Eliezer ben Azariah's Argument: Kerisus (Complete Severance) Cannot Allow Exclusions
The Rabbis' Position: Why They Don't Rely on the Kerisus Argument

macro_segments.title: Conditional Divorce with Temporal Limitations and the Effect of Complete Severance

micro_segments.title: Conditions That Hover Over Her Head: Divorce Valid Only for Limited Time Periods
When Does a Condition Remain Hovering: Lifetime vs. Someone Else's Lifetime
One-Day Divorce: Today Divorced, Tomorrow Married Again
The Principle of Severance: Once Fully Divorced, Always Divorced

macro_segments.title: Conditional Divorce Dependent on the Woman's Marriage to a Specific Person

micro_segments.title: Divorce Conditioned on Marrying Reuven: Preventing Wife-Swapping Appearances
Rav Nachman's Interpretation: She Should Not Marry Reuven, But If She Does, She Stays Married to Him
Rava's Challenge: Can She Marry Someone Else If the Condition Might Never Be Met?
Conditions Beyond the Woman's Control: Comparison to the Sleeping Vow Case
Final Resolution: She Cannot Marry Reuven or Others; If She Does Marry Reuven, She Remains Married to Him

macro_segments.title: Impossible and Forbidden Conditions in Divorce Documents

micro_segments.title: Physically Impossible Conditions: Going to Heaven, Swallowing Giant Sticks, Walking on Water
Rabbi Yehuda ben Teima's Position: Impossible Conditions Are Not Binding
Rav Nachman's Ruling and Support from Mishnah: Impossible Conditions Cannot Be Fulfilled

macro_segments.title: Impossible and Forbidden Conditions in Divorce Documents (continued)

micro_segments.title: The Debate Between Abaye and Rava: Forbidden Acts as Conditions

macro_segments.title: Conditions Involving Forbidden Acts: Eating Pork and Sexual Violations

micro_segments.title: Abaye's Position: Forbidden Acts Cannot Be Conditions Because They Are Forbidden
Rava's Position: Forbidden Acts Are Physically Possible and Therefore Binding Conditions
Sexual Conditions and the Principle of Causality: She Controls Her Own Acts
When Others Must Commit Sin: Father or Biological Father Asked to Violate
Rava's Synthesis: Physical Possibility Plus Control Determines If Condition Is Binding

macro_segments.title: Conditions That Violate Torah Law: Stipulations Against Biblical Obligations

micro_segments.title: Breach of Tanach: Challenge from Tosefta About Binding Conditions Involving Eating Pork
Abaye's Response: The Tosefta Represents the Rabbanan, Not Rabbi Yehuda ben Teima
The Principle of Matne Al Mah Shekatuv BaTorah: Changing the Nature of Torah Institutions
Rav Adda's Clarification: Distinguishing Between Uprooting Institution and Requiring Violation
Ravina's Objection and the Final Distinction: Stipulating Violation vs. Changing the Institution

topical_tags.term: Chutz mi-Ploni (Divorced Except From One Person)
Hafarat Ha-Ba'al (Husband's Power to Void Wife's Vows)
Forbidden Acts as Conditions (Basar Chazir, Drinking Wine)
Matne Al Mah Shekatuv BaTorah (Stipulating Against Torah Law)
She'erah, Kesutah, Ve'Onatah (Wife's Support, Clothing, and Marital Relations)
